title: Congressional Apportionment: A Multiobjective Optimization Approach
abstract: Two events, with major implications for U.S. voters, occur after each decennial census. First, congressional “apportionment” takes place, followed by congressional “districting.” Apportionment determines how to allocate the 435 seats in the House of Representatives across the 50 states, whereas districting determines the geographic boundaries assigned to representatives within each state. Although districting and the practice of gerrymandering often receive great attention in the media and courts, the best way to apportion representatives across states has been debated for nearly 250 years. Historical methods (including the current method) each satisfy some desirable optimality criteria that the others are not guaranteed to satisfy. Moreover, none are guaranteed to optimize certain reasonable fairness measures (e.g., minimum range, minimum bias). To our knowledge, we are the first to formulate and analyze a multiobjective optimization approach to apportionment, allowing policymakers to identify Pareto-optimal allocations and quantify their trade-offs between several competing criteria. Some of these models can be formulated and solved as mixed-integer linear programs, whereas others require the solution of mixed-integer, nonconvex, quadratically constrained quadratic programs. We take advantage of recent software advances that allow one to solve these problems with optimality guarantees. Policy implications of our work include Pareto curves from historical censuses and simulations, which suggest opportunities for improvement in some objectives at little sacrifice to others.

author.Steven M. Shechter: Sauder School of Business, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, British Columbia V6T 1Z2, Canada

article.1. Introduction: Two events, with major implications for U.S. voters, occur after each decennial census. First, congressional “apportionment” takes place, followed by congressional “districting.” Congressional apportionment determines the number of representatives each state receives to serve in the U.S. House of Representatives, and this number remains fixed for the subsequent 10 years. Because of population changes and the democratic goal of proportional representation, each state’s allocation of the 435 total representatives may change from decade to decade. For example, relative to their 2010 allocations, 13 states had a change in representation based on the 2020 census results: Texas gained two seats; Colorado, Florida, Montana, North Carolina, and Oregon each gained one seat; and California, Illinois, Michigan, New York, Ohio, Pennsylvania, and West Virginia each lost one seat (U.S. Census Bureau 2021b). A state’s number of representatives also has great importance for presidential elections, as the number of electoral votes for a state equals its number of representatives plus two—its number of senators.1 After receiving their updated allotment of representatives, state legislatures divide their states into nonoverlapping congressional districts, each represented by one of its allocated representatives. To maintain the principle of “one person, one vote,” each district should be roughly equal in population. Again, because of population changes over the decade, districts may need to be redrawn to meet this requirement (and they certainly need to be redrawn if the state’s total representatives changed from the previous decade). Both Democrat- and Republican-controlled state legislatures have been known to use this redistricting opportunity to game the system for their gain. This process of “gerrymandering” is highly contentious and has led to numerous court cases involving claims of vote dilution (described inNational Conference of State Legislatures 2021,Brennan Center for Justice 2022). This paper focuses on the first decision regarding congressional apportionment. At its core, the problem is one of how best to reconcile the integer requirements of apportionment with the ideal of proportional representation. If each state’s proportion of the U.S. population multiplied by 435 just so happened to be integer, then there would be no debate, as exact proportional representation would be attained. This has never been the case, and almost surely never will be. Although on the surface this may seem to be a trivial matter of rounding, the right way to do this has generated a surprising amount of debate since the infancy of the U.S. government nearly 250 years ago. In 1792, George Washington issued his first presidential veto in response to an apportionment bill (Balinski and Young 1982), and some of the most popular apportionment methods have been proposed by a who’s who of early U.S. history: the (Thomas) Jefferson method, (Alexander) Hamilton method, (Daniel) Webster method, and (John) Adams method. Two other commonly discussed methods, the Dean and Huntington–Hill methods, were proposed by 19th and early 20th century mathematicians. We refer to these methods as the “historical methods,” as they have all been seriously considered and debated in Congress at various times. The Jefferson, Hamilton, and Webster methods were in fact used by Congress in different decades prior to 1940, whereas the Huntington–Hill method has been in use ever since (Balinski and Young 1982). These historical methods are described more inSection 2. Aside from philosophical and theoretical debates on desired properties of apportionment algorithms (discussed more in subsequent sections), what are the practical implications of various methods? To what extent do they lead to different allocations and meaningful differences? Congressional elections (for the House of Representatives) take place every two years and presidential elections every four years. Therefore a state’s decennial apportionment affects the voting power of that state’s population for seven or eight elections every decade (two or three presidential elections depending on whether the census year coincides with a presidential election or not). Across all censuses from 1920 to 2020, there were 285 times out of 542 (53%) in which states would not receive the same number of representatives for all historical methods (i.e., 47% of the time, all methods agreed on a state’s allocation). Although it would be difficult to estimate how these differences might translate to different laws enacted by Congress, the importance of apportionment is especially striking when learning how it may have altered the outcomes of presidential elections. For example, Al Gore would have won the 2000 presidential election if the Jefferson method was used, and he would have tied George W. Bush in electoral votes under the Hamilton method (Lefkowitz 2021). Similarly, the election of Rutherford B. Hayes over Samuel Tilden in the 1876 presidential election also depended on the apportionment method; Tilden would have won under the Hamilton method, but Hayes won under the allocation used at the time (a modified version of the Hamilton method;Balinski and Young 1982). Apportionment outcomes recently made the news after the release of the 2020 census, when it was discovered that if New York had just 89 more residents, or if Minnesota had just 26 fewer residents, New York would have received 27 representatives and Minnesota would have received 7 (instead of 26 and 8, respectively;Goldmacher 2021,Wang 2021). Aside from how population changes could have led to different results, previously used apportionment methods also would have changed New York’s (and other states’) allocations. For example, under the Webster and Hamilton methods, New York would have received one more representative, and under the Jefferson method, it would have received two more representatives. Much of the debate around apportionment centers on notions of fairness, and some of these debates have reached the highest levels of U.S. courts. In 1991 and 1992, Montana and Massachusetts, respectively, sued the U.S. government in federal court, claiming the Huntington–Hill method was unconstitutional (Ernst 1994). Montana argued in favor of using the Adams or Dean methods, whereas Massachusetts argued in favor of using the Webster method. Montana’s arguments centered around measures of variance (arguing for Dean) and range (arguing for Adams). Massachusetts argued that the Huntington–Hill method was unconstitutional because it was biased in favor of smaller states, whereas the Webster method was unbiased. The federal government argued instead that the Huntington–Hill method was unbiased and that the Webster was biased in favor of larger states (Ernst 1994). Although Montana won their case in federal court (Massachusetts lost), the U.S. Supreme Court overturned the decision in 1992, unanimously agreeing that the Huntington–Hill algorithm was constitutional (Ernst 1994). The debate still continues, with some scholars advocating for the Webster method (Young 2001,2004;Balinski 2005) and others proposing a lottery system to deal with fractional apportionments (Grimmett 2004,Procaccia 2021). The historical apportionment methods have two appealing features. First, they are simple. Allocations can be easily calculated on a spreadsheet, and no coding is required. Second, they each optimize some criteria one may expect in a reasonable allocation (discussed more inSection 2andTable 1). However, none of them is guaranteed to satisfy some other desirable objectives, such as minimizing the discrepancy between the most- and least-represented states (i.e., the range), minimizing a weighted sum of deviations from exact proportional representation, or minimizing a measure of bias between smaller and larger states. Furthermore, none of the historical methods provides policymakers with a lever for trading off multiple metrics of interest. We propose multiobjective, mixed-integer programming (MIP) as a methodology for resolving these shortfalls of existing methods. This approach allows policymakers to (1) optimize several alternative objectives not optimized for by current or historical apportionment methods and (2) quantify the trade-offs between competing objectives. By optimizing a weighted combination of the multiple objectives under different sets of weights, one can sketch a Pareto-optimal frontier and identify potential opportunities for making significant gains in one objective at little cost to the others. The rest of this paper is outlined as follows. InSection 2, we review the literature around apportionment methods and the optimality criteria the historical methods satisfy and those they do not. We outline our contributions to this literature inSection 2.1. We present analytical results and counterexamples inSection 3. These findings help explain some of the results of our numerical experiments ofSection 5, which are based on multiobjective optimization formulations presented inSection 4. We provide concluding remarks inSection 6.
article.2. Review of Literature and Historical M: The bookFair RepresentationbyBalinski and Young (1982)provides an excellent history of the debate over apportionment. It also describes various paradoxes and biases the different methods may create, along with desirable mathematical properties they each guarantee. The appendix of the book contains extensive coverage of the mathematical theory of apportionment, with references to several papers on this topic. A recent book byEl-Helaly (2019)also details the mathematics of apportionment. For a concise history of congressional apportionment, including details around the U.S. district court and Supreme Court cases that took place in 1991 and 1992, we refer the reader toErnst (1994). Early mathematical analysis of apportionment was primarily conducted in the 1920s by the mathematician Edward V. Huntington (Huntington 1921a,b;1928a,b).Huntington (1928a)showed that each of the historical methods (except for the Hamilton method) satisfies some pairwise optimality criterion for comparing the congressional representations between any two states. For example, lettingpkandxkbe the population and (integer) number of representatives allocated to statek, respectively, the ideal of proportional representation would mean that between any two statesiandj, their per-capita representation (PCR) would be equal, that is,xipi=xjpj. Because that is highly unlikely, one may instead wish to have the left- and right-hand sides of the equality be “as close as possible.” The meaning of this differs across the historical methods. For example, consider the current method of apportionment, the Huntington–Hill method. As demonstrated inHuntington (1928a), this method leads to an apportionment such that theratioof PCR between any two states (defined as the larger PCR divided by the smaller PCR) cannot be reduced by transferring a representative from the larger-PCR to the smaller-PCR state. Mathematically, it means that the Huntington–Hill apportionment satisfies the following inequality between every two statesiandj: This raises the question of whether and how one might obtain such an apportionment, as the use of Huntington–Hill and other historical methods predates the advent of linear and integer programming. Another major contribution of Huntington was to introduce what he called “working rules” for these historical methods, which were simple algorithms guaranteed to produce apportionments satisfying their respective pairwise optimality criteria (Huntington 1928a, p. 88). For example, by rearranging terms, (1) is equivalent to2 This last inequality forms the basis of the Huntington–Hill algorithm used in practice, which begins by allocating one representative to each state to satisfy the constitutional requirement of at least one representative per state (i.e., begin by settingxi= 1 for each state). It then proceeds by sequentially allocating each next representative to the state with the smallest value ofxi′(xi′+1)pi(wherexi′is the number of representatives assigned to stateithus far in the algorithm), and after the final representative is assigned, the resulting allocation will have the desired pairwise optimality property of (1).Wright (2014,2021) used the Lagrange identity to provide further mathematical justification for the Huntington–Hill method and the sequential algorithm just described. The general public can find the description of this algorithm on the U.S. Census Bureau’s website (note, the Huntington–Hill method is also commonly referred to as the method of equal proportions;U.S. Census Bureau 2021a). Each of the historical methods (except for Hamilton) is pairwise optimal with respect to a comparison ofxipiandxjpjfor any two states. For example, whereas the Huntington–Hill method is pairwise optimal with respect to ratios of PCR, the Webster method is pairwise optimal with respect to the differences; that is, ifxipi≥xjpj, then under the Webster apportionment, transferring a representative fromitojwill not reduce the absolutedifferencebetween the PCR of statesiandj. By performing similar algebraic manipulations, one obtains a simple algorithm for generating a Webster apportionment—give subsequent representatives to the state with the smallest value ofxi′+0.5pi.Table 1summarizes the historical methods, optimality properties they each satisfy, and the sequential algorithms for obtaining the apportionment. Methods 1–5 inTable 1are also known as “divisor” methods. This terminology arises from early apportionment methods (e.g., Jefferson, Adams, Webster), which argued for a divisor,d, along with a fixed rule for rounding eachpidto an integer, such that the sum of rounded values equals the total number of House representatives. The Jefferson method rounds all fractions down, the Adams method rounds all fractions up, and the Webster method rounds all fractions in the usual way (by comparing to 0.5). Although the Huntington–Hill and Dean methods were not originally described as divisor methods, it can be shown that the Huntington–Hill method is equivalent to a divisor method that roundspiddown if it is less than the geometric mean of the two integers it lies between (i.e., ifpid<⌊pid⌋⌈pid⌉) and rounds up otherwise, and the Dean method can be viewed as a divisor method that comparespidto the harmonic mean of its neighboring integers (i.e., it roundspiddown if and only ifpid<2⌊pid⌋⌈pid⌉⌊pid⌋+⌈pid⌉;Balinski and Young 1982).Agnew (2008)expanded the above divisor methods to include ones associated with the logarithmic and identric mean. In a world of fractional representatives, the unique value ofdwould simply be the total population divided by the total number of representatives,d*=PN. Instead, each divisor method results in a range of divisors that works (Balinski and Young 1982). The Hamilton method instead starts with the ideal divisord*and calculates each state’squota,qi=pid*=piPN. A state’s quota represents the fractional number of representatives it would receive under the ideal of proportional representation. The Hamilton method ensures every state receives at least⌊qi⌋, and then gives theN−∑⌊qi⌋remaining representatives one at a time to the states with the largest remaindersqi−⌊qi⌋. The Hamilton method is not considered a divisor method in that the rounding rule depends on all states’ fractional quotas, rather than just the value ofpid. As mentioned,Huntington (1928a)established the pairwise optimality properties and algorithms of divisor methods. The second optimality criteria of the Jefferson and Adams methods were stated (without proof) inBalinski and Young (1982). We provide proofs of equivalent properties inProposition 2ofSection 3.Owens (1921)proved the second optimality criteria of the Webster method. Regarding the third optimality criteria,Balinski and Young (1980, p. 4)defined an apportionment,x, as “near-quota” (or “relatively well-rounded”) if there does not exist two states,iandj, in whichxi<qi−0.5andxj>qj+0.5. They proved that the Webster apportionment is the unique divisor method that has this property (Balinski and Young 1977). The second optimality criteria of the Huntington–Hill method was noted inHuntington (1928a), and the optimality criteria of the Hamilton method was stated inBirkhoff (1976). Each of the optimality criteria listed inTable 1holds under the algorithms indicated provided there are no side constraints, such as the constitutional requirement that every state receives at least one representative.Proposition 1inSection 3clarifies which methods still satisfy the optimality criteria under the requirement of at least one representative per state. A desirable property of the Hamilton method is that it ensures that all states’ representatives arewithin quota(i.e., either equal to⌊qi⌋or⌊qi⌋+1), whereas the divisor methods provide no such guarantee (Balinski and Young 1980). On the other hand, the Hamilton method can give rise to the so-called “Alabama paradox,” which led to Congress abandoning its use for apportionment. The Alabama paradox originated from the discovery that under the Hamilton method of the 1880 census, Alabama could have receivedfewerrepresentatives withmoretotal representatives available for apportionment.3The Hamilton method can also result in the “population” paradox, in which state A grows at a faster rate than state B, yet state A loses a representative to state B. The divisor methods are guaranteed to avoid both the Alabama and population paradoxes (Balinski and Young 1982), but are not guaranteed to stay within quota.Balinski and Young (1974)andSaari (1978)proposed alternative apportionment methods which are both “house monotone” (i.e., avoid the Alabama paradox) and stay within quota. However, as demonstrated in the impossibility result ofBalinski and Young (1980), no apportionment method can both stay within quota and avoid the population paradox. We return to some of these concepts inSection 6. Taking PCR as the focus of comparison between states,4notions of fair allocations may include (1) maximizing the PCR of the worst-off state (i.e.,maxxmini{xipi}), (2) minimizing a measure of discrepancy between the best- and worst-off states (e.g., for differences, minimizing the range of PCRs, or for ratios, minimizing the largest PCR divided by the smallest PCR), or (3) minimizing the largest deviations from a clear reference point (such as the ideal PCR ofNP). As discussed above and shown inTable 1, the Adams method achieves the first notion of fairness. Interestingly, none of the historical methods are guaranteed to satisfy the second or third fairness concepts (discussed further inSection 3).Burt and Harris (1963)suggested that a fair apportionment should minimize the range of PCRs, and they proposed a dynamic programming for achieving this.Gilbert and Schatz (1964)recommended an alternative, polynomial-time algorithm for obtaining a minimum-range allocation, which combines the Adams and Jefferson methods. They also noted that ties for minimum-range allocations were not uncommon. This, in part, motivates our multiobjective optimization approach, as it allows one to break ties by optimizing a secondary objective. In addition to the worst-case focus of the three fairness measures just described, another important fairness concept relates to the smaller- versus larger-state bias concerns mentioned earlier. This has been central to the apportionment debate for over two hundred years. The Jefferson method, the first apportionment algorithm used starting in 1790, was discontinued in 1840 when evidence demonstrated that it was significantly biased in favor of larger states (Balinski and Young 1982). As mentioned earlier, the Massachusetts lawsuit of 1990 hinged on whether the Huntington–Hill or Webster method was less biased. Both sides used data-driven as well as theoretical results to make their case. Further theoretical work on the bias of different apportionment methods has been explored inMarshall et al. (2002),Schuster et al. (2003),Drton and Schwingenschlogl (2005),Schwingenschlogl and Drton (2004), andLauwers and Puyenbroeck (2006a,b). For example,Marshall et al. (2002)used the concept of a majorization partial ordering to show that the historical divisor methods can be ordered from least to most favorable toward larger states as follows: Adams≺Dean≺Huntington–Hill≺Webster≺Jefferson (i.e., the Adams method is most favorable to smaller states while the Jefferson method is most favorable to larger states).Lauwers and Puyenbroeck (2006b)showed that the Hamilton method (which is not a divisor method) sits between the Adams and Jefferson methods in this ordering. We formalize the objective of minimizing smaller- versus larger-state bias in our MIPs ofSection 4and present related numerical results inSections 5.1,5.2, and5.4. A central planner may also want to minimize a total measure (across all states) of deviations from exact proportional representation. Some total deviation metrics are described inTable 1. However, one metric not always minimized by any historical method is the sum of absolute deviations from exact proportional representation, that is,∑i|xipi−NP|. This can easily be accommodated by a MIP approach. Furthermore, becauseNPprovides a clear anchor for states to compare themselves against, the key Prospect Theory concept that utilities arise from comparisons to a reference point is quite relevant (Kahneman and Tversky 1979). Moreover, “loss aversion,” another cornerstone principle of Prospect Theory, might suggest placing a greater penalty on absolute differences for the cases whenxipi<NP(Kahneman and Tversky 1979). Therefore, we generalize the mixed-integer program to minimize∑iwi|xipi−NP|, wherewimay be different for underrepresented versus overrepresented states. We discuss this more inSections 3–5. We conclude this section by noting other applications of MIP and multiobjective optimization to the allocation of indivisible public goods and resources. In fact, another example in the political arena relates to states’ congressional districting decisions, which occur right after the congressional apportionment decisions take place (the focus of our work). As mentioned in the introduction, districting involves states deciding how to allocate their apportioned representatives to geographical catchment areas that cover the state. Multiobjective optimization models of districting appear inMehrotra et al. (1998),Ricca et al. (2013),Swamy et al. (2023), andValidi et al. (2022), among others. Prominent examples outside the political domain include the Nobel Prize–winning work of Alvin Roth on optimal kidney exchanges (Roth et al. 2007) and its various multicriteria extensions (e.g.,Glorie et al. 2014,Biró et al. 2021,Delorme et al. 2023), as well as location and allocation models of affordable housing (e.g.,Johnson and Hurter 1998,Johnson 2007,Zhong et al. 2019,Guo et al. 2020). 2.1. Contributions To our knowledge, multiobjective MIP formulations of apportionment have not appeared in the literature.5Perhaps this is because certain desired optimality criteria are guaranteed through the simpler sequential algorithms of the historical methods (Table 1). However, given the history of debate and the variety of reasonable perspectives on what constitutes an equitable apportionment, it is surprising that their trade-offs have not been formally explored through a multiobjective MIP. For one, as just discussed, policymakers should at least consider whether there are multiple apportionments that tie for one of the methods’ optimality criterion, and then tiebreak with respect to another criterion of interest. Second, even in the absence of ties, a multiobjective optimization approach can reveal opportunities for significant gains in one objective at little sacrifice to others. Our biobjective models allow policymakers to visualize the Pareto-optimal frontier between key metrics to decide whether there is a better middle ground between the extremes of two apportionment methods. Indeed, our figures ofSection 5suggest opportunities for moderate to large easy wins on the Pareto frontier. Our framework is flexible and easily extends to any number of objectives. For example, it can handle any (and all) of the optimality criterion attained by the historical methods. Moreover, it can handle fairness objectives not accounted for by any of the historical methods. For example, a MIP can be formulated to minimize the range of PCR. Perhaps most importantly for the apportionment debates, we create and solve MIPs that formalize the goal of minimizing the bias between smaller and larger states. InSection 3, we present analytical results that will be helpful in understanding some of the numerical results and figures ofSection 5. For example,Proposition 1clarifies which of the historical methods’ pairwise optimality criteria still hold under the minimum requirement of one representative per state. This will help explain why some of our biobjective models (e.g., the Huntington–Hill–based models ofSection 4.2) achieve a zero penalty when all of the weight is put on the pairwise optimality criterion, whereas other models do not attain a zero pairwise penalty (e.g., the Webster-based models ofSection 4.1). The same result, along withProposition 2, will help explain why the Adams method sits at one extreme of the Pareto-optimal curves and why the Jefferson method sits far away from them. Our primary contribution is to raise the possibility of, and demonstrate the potential value of, applying multiobjective optimization to apportionment. We view our modeling frameworks and analyses as policy research tools, which may be useful in future discussions around apportionment. The history of debate and the apportionment lawsuits of the early 1990s have focused entirely on whichsingleapportionment method to implement, based on the fairness concepts they each attain. We propose an alternative viewpoint, which is to use a framework that allows policymakers to blend multiple fairness objectives, moving from a mindset of “either/or” to one that allows “thisandthat.” On the note of lawsuits, as discussed further inSection 6, mathematical models have played key roles “behind the scenes” by both sides in major voting-related court cases. Our work can play a similar role, or perhaps help preempt future lawsuits. Aside from our primary focus on creating policy analysis tools and insights, we believe our problem also offers novel pedagogical benefits in the areas of applied IP and multiobjective optimization. First, it is not common to encountergeneral-integer optimization problems that truly require that a solver restrict the decision variables to be integers.6For example, consider a prototypical production planning problem students often encounter when learning about linear programming (LP). It often involves determining the production quantities of indivisible items, such as windows and tables (see, e.g.,Hillier and Lieberman 2015). Nonetheless, students are taught to solve these problems as LPs and to allow for fractional decision variables, with the idea being that one might either (1) interpret the decision variables to be long-term average rates of production, for which fractional values have meaning, or (2) round the solutions to be integer (if they are not already). Justification for the latter may be that the constraints likely have some flexibility in practice, so that the integer solution can be implemented. However, congressional apportionment involves constraints that are absolutely rigid: (1) states’ apportionments must be integer, and (2) they must sum to 435. Although usual rounding can convert the solution of an LP relaxation to an integer one, there is no guarantee that the resulting apportionments will sum to 435. Also, whereas most multiobjective optimization problems (including the public sector ones mentioned earlier) involve a mix of “efficiency” and “equity” criteria, the congressional apportionment problem is entirely driven by differing notions of “equity.” For example, kidney exchange models may seek to maximize the number of transplants (“efficiency”) while also considering patients’ time spent on dialysis (“equity”;Biró et al. 2021). Political districting models may seek a set of compact districts (“efficiency”) that are also approximately equal in population size (“equity”;Mehrotra et al. 1998). Housing models may seek to maximize social benefits (“efficiency”) while ensuring that each neighborhood has its “fair share” of affordable housing (“equity”;Johnson 2007). On the other hand, the historical debate on congressional apportionment has focused entirely on what constitutes afairallocation of the total representatives.7There is no efficiency or utility measure to maximize in this setting. However, the several defensible views of what a fair apportionment means, and the fact that none of these imply the other, is precisely what motivates a multiobjective optimization approach.
article.3. Analytical Results and Counterexample: This section presents a mix of positive and negative results that bolster the case for a MIP approach to apportionment and help explain solution properties that we may or may not observe in our multiobjective models. As discussed inSection 2,Huntington (1928a)developed the sequential apportionment algorithms for the divisor methods and proved their pairwise optimality conditions. However, although his algorithms assumed each state starts with one representative (as required by the Constitution), the proofs of pairwise optimality did not carefully account for the reason a state may end up with one representative. The proofs implicitly assumed that a state that ends up with a final apportionment of one representative did so because the relevant pairwise comparison finds it better to give it one instead of zero representatives. As we clarify next, this depends on the method. To our knowledge, it has not been previously clarified which optimality criteria still hold under the constitutional requirement of at least one representative per state. All proofs appear in the appendix. (a)The pairwise optimality criteria of the Huntington–Hill, Adams, and Dean methods still hold under the constraint that each state must receive at least one representative.(b)The pairwise optimality criteria of the Jefferson and Webster methods may be violated under the requirement of at least one representative per state. Proposition 1(a) explains why the numerical results of our biobjective MIPs ofSection 5always show a zero penalty when putting all of the weight on the Huntington–Hill pairwise objective, whereasProposition 1(b) explains why the same cannot be said when putting all of the weight on the Webster pairwise objective. Although the Jefferson and Webster methods may violate the pairwise optimality condition under the minimum of one representative per state, a corollary of the above proposition is that it is only between states having one representative each where this violation may occur. Under the minimum requirement of one representative per state, any violations of the pairwise optimality conditions of the Jefferson and Webster methods may only occur between two states both receiving one representative in the final allocation. Before analyzing the fairness measures described above, we pivot from comparing apportionments based on states’ PCR (i.e.,xipi) to comparing them based on their representatives as a fraction of their quota (i.e.,xiqi, whereqi=piPN); that is, we will compare states based on theirper quota representation(from here on,PQR). We do this for three reasons: (1) our optimization models will produce the same optimal solutions under PCR- or PQR-based comparisons, (2) the PQR-based formulations greatly improve the scaling of the mixed-integer programs (we discuss this more inSection 4), and (3) a state’s quota has been a focal point of past discussions around apportionment (recall the “within-quota” property of the Hamilton method and the “near-quota” property of the Webster method). Regarding the equivalence of optimizing under PCR- or PQR-based comparisons, note that a state’s PQR is just a constant multiple (PN) of its PCR; that is,xiqi=PN(xipi). Given that a state’s quota provides a natural anchor for what they might reasonably expect to receive, it would likewise be natural for a state to consider the fraction its apportionment is above or below quota. Toward that end, we define the following: We refer toδi,Uandδi,Oas the “underage” and “overage,” respectively, for a state, that is, the (nonnegative) fraction by which a state’s apportionment is under or over quota. First, we consider the fairness concept of maximizing the PQR of the worst-off state. This is equivalent to minimizing the maximum underage of any state. Our next result shows that the Adams method achieves this. It also establishes that the Jefferson method minimizes the maximum overage. In the statements of the proposition,xrepresents any feasible apportionment, andArepresents the set of all feasible apportionments. (Maximum Fractional Deviations from Quota). (a)The Adams method minimizes the maximum underage over all states; that is, it attainsminx∈Amaxiδi,U. (b)The Jefferson method minimizes the maximum overage over all states; mathematically, it attainsminx∈Amaxiδi,O. The above statements hold under both no minimum, as well as a minimum of one representative per state. For example, the sequential Jefferson method, which begins by first allocating one representative to each state, minimizes the maximum overage, subject to the requirement of at least one representative per state; similarly, a Jefferson method that begins by assigning zero representatives to each state (and then allocating sequentially based on the ranking rule shown in the third column ofTable 1), minimizes the maximum overage subject to no minimum representation required per state. However, the Jefferson method might achieve a lower objective value by eliminating the constraint of at least one representative per state, whereas the Adams method would not benefit from this relaxation. This is stated as the following corollary. (a)The Adams method always attains the same maximum underage value with or without the requirement of one representative per state.(b)The Jefferson method may attain a smaller maximum overage value by reducing the minimum number of representatives per state from one to zero. The corollary follows fromPropositions 1and2and is a consequence of the fact that the Adams method begins by giving one representative to each state by the nature of its sequential ranking algorithm (“give the next representative to the state with the smallest value ofxipi”), whereas the Jefferson ranking algorithm (“give next representative to the state with the smallest value ofxi+1pi”) might never give a very low-population state a representative (unless required to do so). Note that the Adams method may very well terminate with some states receiving just one representative, and therefore the constraint of at least one representative per state is binding in that case. However, unlike linear programs, which often benefit by relaxing binding constraints, part (a) of the corollary indicates that this will never be the case when minimizing the maximum underage. However, part (b) suggests that relaxing the binding constraint of at least one representative per state can be beneficial when minimizing the maximum overage (it will depend on the state populations for a particular decade). One may also wish to minimize the maximum value of either type of deviation, negative or positive, that is, tominx∈Amaxi|δi|. Furthermore, in 100 simulated populations described inSection 5, we find that the Webster method matches the optimized allocation each time. One may expect this from the Webster method, in light ofBalinski and Young’s (1980)result that the Webster method satisfies the “near-quota” property. However,Ernst (1994, table 9) provided a counterexample to show that the Webster is not guaranteed to minimize the maximumδi, and therefore Webster is not necessarily a method that is nearest the quota in this sense. article.4. Biobjective Optimization Formulations: Here we formulate multiobjective optimization models, which allow policymakers to understand how different allocations compare over multiple outcomes of interest. For ease of exposition and visualization, we present a set of biobjective models. By putting different weights on the two objectives, policymakers can sketch out a partial Pareto frontier and decide which points on the curves they prefer. One objective of our models will measure proximity to the pairwise optimality criterion of two historical methods: Webster (Section 4.1) and Huntington–Hill (Section 4.2). We focus on these two methods because most of the debate in the past century has been around whether to use Webster or Huntington–Hill. In the early 1900s, Wilcox argued for the Webster method, whereas Huntington argued for his method, and in the Supreme Court case of 1992, Young argued for the Webster method while Ernst argued for Huntington–Hill (Balinski and Young 1980,Ernst 1994). The second objective will measure (in separate models) various notions of fairness discussed earlier: (1) the maximum underage (i.e., the least-represented state), (2) the range, (3) the maximum absolute fractional deviation from the quota, (4) the bias between smaller and larger states, and (5) the weighted total deviations from exact proportional PCR. As we shall see below, the Webster-based formulations can be solved as mixed-integerlinearprograms, whereas the Huntington–Hill–based formulations lead to mixed-integernonconvex quadratically constrained quadratic programs(QCQPs). The primary decision variables for each model are denoted byxi, the integer number of representatives assigned to statei, and we assume the total number of representatives to apportion (N) equals 435 (this has been the value ofNsince 1920). 4.1. Webster-Based Formulations We begin by presenting the formulation for the mixed-integer program that trades off the pairwise optimality criterion of the Webster method against the maximum underage of any state. In addition to the apportionment decision variablesxi, we let slack variableyrepresent the maximum deviation from the Webster pairwise optimality condition, and we let slack variablevrepresent the maximum underage for a given allocation. The objective is to minimize the weighted combination ofwvv+wyy, wherewvandwyare nonnegative weights. To ensurevandytake on the desired metrics, we include appropriate constraints. Inequalities (7) ensure thatvis bounded below by the largest underage. As shown inProposition 2, the Adams method also achieves this objective. Inequalities (8) result from the pairwise optimality condition of the Webster method, which is to allocate representatives so that the difference in PQR (or PCR) between any two statesiandjcannot be reduced by transferring a representative from stateito statej. In other words, ifxiqi≥xjqj, then the following must hold: The right-hand side of (8) is the difference between the left- and right-hand sides of (11), and by allowing the nonnegative slack variableyon the left-hand side of (8), we soften the Webster pairwise optimality condition. To formulate the “Webster versus Maximum Absolute Deviation” model, we take the Webster versus Maximum Underage model and include the following additional constraints: Using a positive value forwvin combination withConstraints (7)and (14) ensures thatvtakes the value of the maximum absolute deviation for a given allocation. We next consider a smaller- versus larger-state bias objective by applying a measure of bias proposed byBalinski and Young (1982), which was also used for comparing the Huntington–Hill and Webster methods in the Massachusetts court cases. The idea is to create two sets of states,SandL, with|S|=|L|and such thatpi<pjfor eachi∈Sandj∈L. In other words,Sis a set of smaller-population states, andLis a set of larger-population states. Then, a method’s apportionment vectorxis said to be biased in favor of smaller states if Therefore, to formulate the “Webster versus Absolute Bias” model, we consider the goal of an unbiased apportionment in which the left- and right-hand sides of (15) are equal. We soften this constraint by lettingvrepresent the absolute value of their difference. That is, we replaceConstraints (7)from the initial formulation with the following two linear constraints: In our numerical study ofSection 5, we will follow the convention used inBalinski and Young (1982), which is to have|S|=|L|=⌊numberofstates3⌋. There were 48 states in the United States for the 1920–1950 apportionments (before Alaska and Hawaii became states) and 50 states starting with the 1960 census; therefore, we letSbe the set of states with the 16 smallest quotas andLbe the set of states with the 16 largest quotas for each census from 1920 to 2020. Also, unlike the approaches taken byBalinski and Young (1982)(who excluded fromSany states with quotas less than 0.5) andErnst (1994)(who excluded any states with quotas less than one), we do not exclude any states fromS. Their motivation for excluding some or all states with quotas less than one was to limit the small-state bias of the constitutional requirement of at least one representative per state. Instead, we take this as a reality under which all apportionment methods operate, and our bias objective seeks to still balance the bias (as much as possible) under this reality. Finally, we also consider an alternative metric that sums weighted deviations across all states. For example, as discussed inSection 2, none of the historical methods guarantees minimizing∑i|δi|. We generalize this by formulating a biobjective MIP formulation of “Webster versus Weighted Sum of Deviations” as follows: This formulation allows one to create underage (vi,u) and overage (vi,o) deviations for each state (Inequalities (20)and (21), respectively). By defining thevobjective value inEquality (17), using nonnegative weightswi,uandwi,0for alli, and ensuring nonnegativity ofvi,uandvi,ovia (23), at most one ofvi,uandvi,uwill be positive for each stateiin an optimal solution (with the other value or both being zero). The sum of these for eachithen represents that state’s absolute fractional deviation from quota, whether it is under- or overrepresented. The same formulation also allows policymakers to capture the behavioral concept of loss aversion, which may be a useful strategy for hedging against the risk of states bringing lawsuits against the government for underrepresentation. For example, Massachusetts and Montana, the two states that sued the federal government in 1991 (Ernst 1994), both had allocations less than their quota under the Huntington–Hill method, but would have both had allocations above quota under a 2:1 penalty for underage versus overage (in the spirit of loss aversion from Prospect Theory). We discuss this more inSection 5. Finally, although we frame the MIP models as biobjective models, by settingwv>0andwy= 0 in each variant, one is able to fully optimize any one of these alternative metrics to the Webster optimality criterion. Although the Adams method minimizes the maximum underage, none of the historical methods is guaranteed to minimize the other four objectives we consider. 4.2. Huntington–Hill–Based Formulations The formulations here are analogous to the Webster ones, but withydesigned to be a measure of deviation from the pairwise optimality criterion of Huntington–Hill. Recall that this pairwise condition states that the ratio of PCRs (or, equivalently, PQRs) between any two states (expressed as the larger PCR divided by the smaller PCR) cannot be reduced by transferring a representative from the larger-PCR state to the smaller-PCR state. This is represented by inequality (1). To soften this for the biobjective model, we introduce the following initial constraints (in PQR form) for alli≠j: By using the sameyvariable in (24) for all pairs of statesiandj, in combination with a positive weightwyin the objective function,ycan be interpreted as the maximum fractional reduction in the PQR ratio between any two states that can be achieved by transferring a representative from the larger-PQR state to the smaller-PQR state. Whenwv= 0 andwy>0, we recover the Huntington–Hill allocation, in whichy= 0. By cross-multiplying the denominators on the left- and right-hand sides of (24) (assumingxi≠1; seeEndnote 2) and rearranging terms, we obtain the equivalent inequality Whereas the right-hand side of (25) is quadratic, the left-hand side involves a term of degree three (yxi2). Moreover, the constraint is nonconvex. We use variable substitutions to keep the formulation quadratic and greatly reduce the number of nonconvex constraints. This reformulation allows us to take advantage of solution algorithms with global optimality guarantees for QCQPs, even when some constraints are nonconvex (see, e.g.,Horst 1976,Raber 1998,Linderoth 2005,Burer and Vandenbussche 2006). In particular, we solve our models withGurobi (2021), which has recently added the ability to solve nonconvex QCQPs. Lines (30)–(32) together are equivalent to (25). The variablesaiandbidefined in (30) and (31) create nonconvex quadratic constraints, but there are only 100 of them (50 for theaiand 50 for thebi), instead of the 2,450 (49 × 50) nonconvex (and nonquadratic) constraints of (25). The 2,450 pairwise constraints of the reformulation are now expressed as linear constraints, via (32). To solve the Huntington–Hill–based formulations using the metrics of range, maximum absolute deviation, bias, or weighted sum of deviations, we can follow the same reformulations of thev-constraints described inSection 4.1for the Webster-based versions.
article.5. Numerical Results: We obtained data for each census between 1920 and 2020 from the U.S. Census website. State-by-state population counts used for the purposes of apportionment8from 1990 to 2020 are available in Excel format atwww.census.gov/topics/public-sector/congressional-apportionment/data.html. Population counts for the 1920–1980 censuses are available in PDF files atwww.census.gov/history/pdf/ApportionmentInformation-XCensus.pdf(replacing “X” with the decade of interest; e.g. 1980). We manually entered the values obtained there. 5.1. Webster-Based Results We begin by presenting the results of the Webster versus Maximum Underage metric (the findings and insights regarding the range and maximum deviation are similar). Because the Adams method produces an apportionment that minimizes the maximum underage, the solutions of our biobjective models trace Pareto-optimal curves between the Adams apportionment and the Webster/Huntington–Hill apportionments. We obtain other (not necessarily all) possible Pareto-optimal apportionments by solving the models withwv∈{0,0.001,0.05,0.10,0.15,…,0.85,0.90,0.95,0.999,1}andwy=1−wv. Exact optimal solutions for these problems (i.e., with tolerance gap set to zero in Gurobi) were quickly obtained for each MIP (i.e., for a given combination of (wv,wy) and for a given census), taking an average of 0.7 seconds and a maximum of 2.3 seconds. Figure 1shows the averaged trade-offs (over the 1920–2020 censuses) between deviations from the pairwise optimality criterion of the Webster method (y-axis) and the maximum underage of any state (x-axis), for each of the historical allocation methods and different weightings of the Webster-based biobjective optimization model. As expected, the weightingwv= 1 andwy= 0 returns the Adams allocation (the circle indicating this is covered by the square for Adams inFigure 1), whereaswv= 0 andwy= 1 returns the Webster apportionment. Note that in general, putting all of the weight on one objective and zero weight on the other in a biobjective optimization model does not necessarily return a Pareto-optimal solution. This can happen when there are ties for optimal solutions. For the Adams and Webster methods, ties would be extremely rare; for example, they may arise if some states had the exact same populations or populations that were specific multiples of each other. On the other hand, ties are not uncommon for apportionments that minimize the range (this is discussed inHuntington (1928a)andBurt and Harris (1963)). Therefore, for biobjective models using the range fairness concept, one should not assume that solving the model with(wv=1,wy=0)necessarily results in a Pareto-optimal solution. Instead, a Pareto-optimal apportionment that minimizes the range can be obtained using a two-step process. In the first step, one solves the model with(wv=1,wy=0)(which minimizes the range), and in the second step, one minimizesysubject to a constraint thatvequals the minimum range obtained from the first step. There are a number of other observations to highlight regardingFigure 1. First, the Pareto curve is nearly horizontal near the Webster allocation. This suggests that there are opportunities for significant reduction of the maximum underage metric at very little increase to the pairwise deviation penalty. Therefore, the Webster method may be “missing out” on opportunities for easy improvements to other fairness criteria. Second, most of the historical methods appear close to the Pareto frontier, and the Huntington–Hill method appears to offer reductions in underage with little sacrifice to the Webster pairwise penalty. Third, the Jefferson method performs poorly on these trade-offs. Recall that the Jefferson method’s goal is in some sense opposite that of the Adams method, in that it tries to minimize the maximumoverageinstead of underage. Moreover, as indicated inCorollary 2, it may be prevented from further lowering this metric because of the requirement of at least one representative per state. Indeed, in experiments removing this requirement, the Jefferson method performs much better than it does with the requirement. Fourth, the Webster allocations do not in general achieve a zero penalty for the pairwise criterion. The reason for this also relates toProposition 1(b), which states that the Webster pairwise optimality criterion may be violated under the minimum requirement of one representative per state. While some decades achieve a zero penalty fory, others may not. Although the averaged results may provide a sense of what is possible, it does not mean that easy wins on the Pareto curves will appear in every census. For example, whereas 1930 offered great reductions in the maximum underage for negligible deterioration in the pairwise penalty (Figure 2(a)), 2010 offered a modest reduction in maximum underage for a small increase on they-axis (Figure 2(b)), and 2020 did not present any easy trade-offs (Figure 2(c)). Trade-off curves for each decade appear in the Online Appendix, in Figure A.1. We also examined how sensitive trade-offs are to the population counts of a given census. For example, although the exact population counts from 2020 do not exhibit opportunities for easy wins, would these arise under slight changes to the 2020 populations? Toward this end, we simulated 100 independent populations, in which each simulated population is obtained by taking each state’s 2020 population and adding a uniform random deviation of ±10%. The averaged results over these 100 sampled populations appear inFigure 3. Here we do see a considerably flat region near the Webster allocation, suggesting that the importance of considering these trade-offs is still relevant for current populations. We next illustrate how different points on the Pareto frontier translate into actual differences in states’ allocations. Using the 2010 census as an example (Figure 2(b)), the Webster allocation (equivalent to solving the biobjective optimization with weightswv= 0 andwy= 1) results in a maximum underage of 0.326. This results from Rhode Island receiving one representative but having a quota of 1.49. We also see that the Webster penalty is zero for this decade, indicating that it does achieve the pairwise optimality ideal despite this not being guaranteed in general. The next point to the left of the Webster allocation (obtained from a weighting ofwv=0.3andwy=0.7) reduces the maximum underage to 0.285, while increasing the Webster pairwise penalty to 0.014. Relative to the Webster apportionment, the new apportionment results from North Carolina transferring one representative to Rhode Island. Now the least represented state is Montana, having one representative for a quota of 1.40. Moving to the other extreme of the Pareto curve (i.e., the Adams apportionment) leads to the minimum maximum underage of 0.05, which in this case is experienced by Pennsylvania, with 17 representatives for a quota of 17.92. The pairwise Webster penalty in this case is 0.68, which results by comparing the apportionments for the states of California and South Dakota. Under the Adams allocation, South Dakota’s PQR is 1.73 (2 representatives for a quota of 1.15), whereas California’s PQR is 0.95 (50 representatives for a quota of 52.54), for a difference of 0.78. However, if South Dakota transferred one of its representatives to California, California’s PQR would increase to 0.97, whereas South Dakota’s PQR would decrease to 0.87, resulting in a PQR difference of 0.10. In other words, the pairwise difference in PQR between California and South Dakota can be reduced by 0.68 (from a difference of 0.78 to 0.10), matching the penalty indicated on they-axis. Next, we consider the Webster versus Absolute Bias objective. Note that the sole objective of obtaining an allocation that minimizes bias will have many ties, including clearly nonsensical apportionments. For example, giving a weight of one to the bias objective and a weight of zero to the Webster objective for the 2020 census returns an optimal solution that gives 188 representatives to the largest 16 states and 18 representatives to the smallest 16 states (for a minimized bias objective of7.98×10−5). However, the solution the solver returns gives 173 representatives to Tennessee (the 16th largest state) and one each to the other 15 larger states. This can happen because the optimal solution does not care how the 188 total representatives are distributed across those 16 states. Therefore, a secondary objective of importance (such as the Webster criterion) is indeed needed in this case.Figure 4shows the trade-off curve between these two objectives.9 There are a number of things worth highlighting about the Webster versus Absolute Bias analysis. First, focusing on just the bias metric alone, we observe the same ordering relation described inMarshall et al. (2002), namely, that the average bias of the historical divisor methods is ordered from least to most favorable to larger states as Adams≺Dean≺Huntington–Hill≺Webster≺Jefferson.Figure 4shows average absolute bias, but the Jefferson method was the only divisor method favorable to larger states (with a negative bias of −0.18), whereas the other methods were all favorable to smaller states. Second, if one is generally pro-Webster but also cares about bias, the Pareto curve again suggests opportunities for improvements on the latter metric without much sacrifice on the Webster criterion. Figure A.2 of the Online Appendix shows the trade-off curves by decade, with the years 1950 and 1980 demonstrating these types of opportunities. 5.2. Huntington–Hill–Based Results As noted above, the Webster method may not achieve a zero penalty on the pairwise objective. On the other hand, in accordance withProposition 1(a), the Huntington–Hill method necessarily achieves a zero penalty on the pairwise criterion, even under the requirement of one representative per state. We see this with they-value of zero in the Pareto curve of the averaged results from the 1920–2020 censuses (Figure 5) for the biobjective optimization that puts all of the weight ony(which results in the same apportionment as the Huntington–Hill apportionment). ComparingFigures 1and5also suggest that the Huntington–Hill method performs better (on average) on the Webster-based penalty compared with how the Webster method performs on the Huntington–Hill–based penalty. Analogous toFigure 2,Figure 6shows that the degree of trade-off between the Huntington–Hill pairwise penalty and the maximum underage again may manifest quite differently for different decades. Relative to the Huntington–Hill apportionment, the results of the 2000 census (Figure 6(a)) indicate the possibility of a significant decrease in the maximum underage (from 0.29 to 0.18) for a small increase in the Huntington–Hill pairwise penalty (from 0 to 0.018). On the other hand, 2020 (Figure 6(b)) just shows two Pareto-optimal points, one that sits at the Huntington–Hill apportionment extreme of the lower right versus one that sits at the Adams extreme of the upper left. The results for each decade appear in the Online Appendix, in Figure A.3. Recall that the Huntington–Hill method has been used to apportion representatives for every census since 1940. For the 2000 census, starting from the Huntington–Hill apportionment, the Pareto-optimal point that decreases the maximum underage by 0.11 for an increased pairwise penalty of 0.018 results from North Carolina transferring one of its 13 representatives to Montana and its 1 representative (this apportionment results from a weighting ofwv=0.15andwy=0.85). By doing so, the least-represented state switches from Montana (which under the Huntington–Hill apportionment has a PQR of 0.71) to Delaware (with a PQR of 0.82). This explains the reduction from the maximum underage of 0.29 under the Huntington–Hill method to the maximum underage of 0.18 as thex-value of this new point. The increase in the pairwise penalty can be understood as follows. Under the Huntington–Hill method, the ratio of PQRs between North Carolina and Montana is1.0420.715=1.459, whereas after the transfer, the ratio is1.4290.962=1.485, meaning the latter can be reduced by 1.8% by undoing the transfer, hence the 0.018 penalty plotted on they-axis. Moving to the other extreme Pareto-optimal solution of the Adams solution, the maximum underage for any state results from New York receiving 28 representatives for a quota of 29.38 (a PQR of 0.953, resulting in the underage of 0.047 for thex-value). The penalty on they-axis results from the pairwise comparison of California and South Dakota. Under the Adams apportionment, South Dakota’s PQR is 1.71 (2 representatives for a quota of 1.17), whereas California’s PQR is 0.95 (50 representatives for a quota of 52.45), for a ratio of 1.79. However, if South Dakota transferred one of its representatives to California, the latter would have a PQR of 0.97, and South Dakota would have a PQR of 0.85, resulting in a PQR ratio of 1.14. This would be a 36.6% decrease from the ratio of 1.71, resulting in they-axis value of 0.37 for the Adams allocation. Finally, we compare the Huntington–Hill objective with the Absolute Bias objective inFigure 7(analogous toFigure 4for the Webster method). Similar to our findings with the Webster case, we see here and in the decade-based results in Figure A.4 of the Online Appendix that even if one is generally an advocate of the Huntington–Hill method, there may be easy wins on reducing the smaller- versus larger-state bias without much sacrifice to the Huntington–Hill ideal. 5.3. Weighted Sums of Deviations from Quota Here we describe some results regarding the objective of minimizing a weighted sum of deviations, that is,∑iwi|δi|. First, we considerwi= 1 for alli. Interestingly, the resulting allocation of this stand-alone (i.e., not biobjective) optimization problem matches the Webster allocation for every census between 1920 and 2020. Both allocations are also within quota for each of those decades. Recall that the Webster method is not in general guaranteed to be within quota nor guaranteed to minimize∑i|δi|. Both properties also hold for the Webster allocation that results from the 100 sampled populations described above. The Huntington–Hill method, although also within quota for every census from 1920 to 2020, did not minimize∑i|δi|for every decade. However, its average performance over that span was nearly identical to that of the Webster method (0.0825 versus 0.0815 averaged per-state|δi|for Huntington–Hill versus Webster, respectively). Next, we incorporate the Prospect Theory idea of loss aversion by settingwi= 2 for states with negative values ofδiandwi= 1 for states with positive values. In this case, the Huntington–Hill method (with an average weighted per-state deviation of 0.1083) comes closer to the optimal minimum (0.1057) compared with the Webster method (0.1121). This can be explained through our previous discussions and findings on how the Huntington–Hill and Webster methods compare on measures of small- versus large-state bias. As noted inSection 2,Marshall et al. (2002)proved a partial ordering on bias among the historical divisor methods, showing that the Huntington–Hill method is more biased in favor of smaller states compared with the Webster method. Numerically, we found that the Huntington–Hill method has an average bias of 0.055 in favor of the smaller states, whereas the Webster method has an average bias of 0.031 in favor of smaller states (Figure 4). Because smaller states are more prone to larger proportional deviations compared with larger states (because of their smaller denominators), the Huntington–Hill method incurs less overall underage and the larger loss-aversion multiplier that comes with it. 5.4. Maximum Underage vs. Bias Whereas the previous subsections presented trade-offs between different fairness concepts and the current allocation method (Huntington–Hill) and the formerly used Webster method, our final set of experiments do not assume any anchoring or preference for either of these historical methods. Instead, we examine here the direct trade-off between two of the fairness measures explored above: the maximum underage across states and the absolute bias between smaller and larger states.Figure 8shows the averaged results over the 1920–2020 censuses. As discussed previously, putting all of the weight on the underage objective leads to the Adams apportionment, as shown in the lower-right extreme of the Pareto curve. From this position, one can reduce the average absolute bias without much harm to the worst-off state. On the other hand, it may be more difficult for a decision maker to decide where to sit on the Pareto curve for the weights that lead to an average absolute bias of less than 0.05. Interestingly, though, several of these points dominate both the Huntington–Hill and Webster methods. For example, when putting a weight of 0.25 on the maximum underage and 0.75 on the absolute bias, the averaged results yield the plotted point of (absolute bias, maximum underage) =(0.0019,0.2309), whereas the Webster and Huntington–Hill methods yield(0.0287,0.2734)and(0.0587,0.2309), respectively. Figure A.5 of the Online Appendix shows the decade-by-decade results. The same weighting of 0.25 and 0.75 yields dominant solutions over both the Webster and Huntington–Hill methods in 7 of the 11 censuses between 1920 and 2020.
article.6. Discussion: The nearly 250-year-old debate around congressional apportionment appears entirely focused on whichsingleproposed method to use. The literature has shown how the different historical methods optimize different objectives. Although some of the methods may result in the same apportionments in some decades, none of them in general implies the other, and thus their objectives may compete with each other. Therefore, we propose multiobjective optimization as a means for combining multiple methods and their respective goals. As demonstrated in our numerical experiments, this approach may reveal opportunities to improve significantly on one objective at little sacrifice to another. Political philosopher JohnRawls’ (1971)Theory of Justiceespoused a maximin concept of fairness, in which the least advantaged individual in society should be made as best off as possible. This is precisely what the Adams apportionment method achieves. Surprisingly, it does not appear that any states have used this fact to argue for the Adams method, and Congress has never used it for apportionment. In their district court case arguing against the constitutionality of the Huntington–Hill method, Montana did state that the Adams or the Dean methods would be fairer. However, their basis for supporting the Adams method was not that it maximizes the minimum PCR, but rather that it led to a smaller range of district sizes across states in 1990 (Ernst 1994). In biobjective models, we focused on the trade-off between several fairness concepts and the pairwise-optimality criteria of two historical methods debated in the courts and literature—the currently used Huntington–Hill method and the formerly used Webster method. The debate between these methods was the focus of the Massachusetts District Court case of 1990 (Ernst 1994). Also, as noted inSections 2and4, mathematicians have debated the merits of Huntington–Hill versus Webster. Our results show that whether one is pro-Huntington–Hill or pro-Webster, a multiobjective optimization approach allows policymakers to relax the traditional single-objective focus of these methods and find potentially significant gains in other fairness metrics. Although we focused on biobjective models for ease of exposition, a mathematical programming framework can easily accommodate any number of objectives. For example, one can combine weighted deviations from the Webster and Huntington–Hill pairwise optimality criteria along with the weighted maximum underage in a three-objective model. Another advantage of a mathematical programming approach to apportionment is its ability to include various side constraints. As discussed inSection 2, the mathematical literature on apportionment has shown that divisor methods avoid the Alabama paradox (as well as other paradoxes), whereas the Hamilton method may not. On the other hand, the Hamilton method stays within quota, whereas the divisor methods may not. With an optimization approach, side constraints can be included to ensure apportionments stay within quota. For example, to enforce within quota, we can associate a binary variable,yi, with each state and include the constraintxi=⌊qi⌋yi+(⌊qi⌋+1)(1−yi)for alli. Alternatively, if one wants to relax the within-quota restriction but still ensure that each stateiis at mostuibelow quota and at mostoiabove quota, this is easily handled by including the constraintsxi≥qi−uiandxi≤qi+oi. A point of contention policymakers may raise with a multiobjective optimization-based approach to apportionment is that it can produce the Alabama paradox. Recall that this possibility effectively ended Congress’s use of the Hamilton method for apportionment. On the other hand, this quirk cannot arise with the current method (as well as any divisor method). However, we would argue that the flexibility of side constraints may help alleviate this concern, practically (albeit not theoretically) avoiding the Alabama paradox. For example, suppose that Congress decides to increase the total number of representatives in the House from 435 toM. Instead of immediately running the multiobjective optimization by updating the constraint∑i=150xi=435to∑i=150xi=Mand resolving, we can take an iterative approach, by first solving the optimization model with the constraint∑i=150xi=435. Labeling the optimal apportionment decisions of this instance asxi,435, we proceed to the next optimization iteration, in which we set∑i=150xi=436and include the additional constraints thatxi≥xi,435for alli. This effectively guards against the Alabama paradox, as it ensures a state cannot receive fewer representatives when the total available for allocation increases. We can continue in this iterative manner, until we solve the version with∑i=150xi=M, along with the constraintsxi≥xi,M−1. We conclude with a discussion on the potential use of our models. As noted earlier, the historical methods are appealing for their simplicity; indeed, they can be set up easily in a spreadsheet. On the other hand, the multiobjective optimization approach requires running specialized optimization algorithms. Although solutions are obtained in seconds, the solution algorithms themselves (e.g., simplex and branch and bound) are not easily explained or interpretable. This is could be a potential barrier to implementation. However, there is precedent for governments using mathematical optimization and commercial solvers in other important areas. For example, the U.S. Federal Communications Commission (FCC), a governmental agency appointed by Congress to regulate radio, television, and wireless services, has developed sophisticated optimization-based solutions of auctions designed to repurpose television stations for wireless use. In fact, the FCC team won the prestigious 2018 Franz Edelman Award from the Institute for Operations Research and Management Sciences for this work (INFORMS 2018). The government of Chile won the same award the following year for their use of various operations research methodologies (including mathematical optimization) to improve prediction and resource allocation during the COVID-19 pandemic (Basso et al. 2023). In order to use our models, policymakers would first need to decide which objectives to include and then decide their relative importance. This may be expressed through different weights or through explicit constraints on some objectives while optimizing others. For example, if Congress wanted to minimize the Huntington–Hill objective subject to a hard limit on the performance degradation of some other objective (e.g., the deviation from the Webster or Adams ideal), they could easily use these models to obtain such a solution.10 Interestingly, the U.S. government does not indicate what to do in the event of a tie when using the Huntington–Hill method.11Granted, this is very unlikely to happen; however, it is not impossible. Huntington himself discussed this possibility and casually suggested giving preference to the state with the larger population (Huntington 1928a). Even assuming Congress wants to continue with the Huntington–Hill method indefinitely, if a tie for the final representative ever arose, it would face an important decision on how to break the tie. Consider the following example, in which one more representative remains (i.e., the 435th representative) to be allocated via the sequential Huntington–Hill algorithm described inTable 1. Suppose statesiandj, with different populationspiandpjhave been allocatedxi′andxj′representatives thus far, and supposexi′(xi′+1)pi=xj′(xj′+1)pj=mink{xk′(xk′+1)pk}. In other words, the final representative should go to either stateior statej. Although either choice is equally optimal from a Huntington–Hill perspective, that may not be the case for secondary objectives of interest. Let us consider a very small, simple example for illustration. Assume there are only two states, 1 and 2, withp1=1andp2=6, and a total of 10 representatives for allocation. The Huntington–Hill method begins by allocating one representative to each state. It is easy to verify that the sequential method will allocate the next seven representatives to state 2, bringing it tox2′=8, with state 1 still havingx1′=1. At this point, one representative remains to be allocated. However, we find thatx1′(x1′+1)p1=1(2)1=8(9)6=x2′(x2′+1)p2, and hence we have a tie. Therefore, we now need to decide between two final allocations: (1)x1=1,x2=9or (2)x1=2,x2=8. Suppose that the government also cares about the Rawlsian maximin fairness concept. Now we shall see that the two solutions imply different values on this dimension. The smallest value ofxipifrom tiebreak solution number 1 yields11(from state 1), whereas the smallest PCR from tiebreak solution number 2 is43(from state 2). Hence, the government should prefer solution 2. On the other hand, if it followed Huntington’s suggestion of giving preference to the state with the larger population, then it would have gone with solution 1. The motivation for our work was to develop modeling frameworks, analyses, and insights that may be useful in future policy debates and discussions about congressional apportionment. Aside from direct implementation, mathematical models have been used behind the scenes in major court cases related to voting. As mentioned inSection 1and detailed inErnst (1994), applied mathematicians were hired to represent both the plaintiffs12and the defendants13in federal and Supreme Court cases about apportionment. Arguments and counterarguments involved claims of which algorithms optimized which objectives. As noted inSection 1, congressional redistricting is an especially contentious voting rights issue often litigated in state, federal, and Supreme Court cases. Although it is difficult to ascertain to what extent different states are implementing mathematical optimization models to generate their districts, again we see the important role such models play in legal arguments and courts’ decisions. For example, applied mathematicians’ testimonies were key to discussions and findings that the congressional maps created by North Carolina14and Pennsylvania15were gerrymandered (Hershberger 2022). Our models and results could potentially help the government avoid future lawsuits. For example, returning to the maximin principle as a fairness concept, Montana in 2000 could have referred to the example fromFigure 6and the discussion ofSection 5.2to argue that it should receive two representatives (instead of one under the Huntington–Hill method) and North Carolina should receive 12 representatives (instead of 13), leading to the plotted point (0.18, 0.018) instead of the Huntington–Hill point (0.29, 0). This could be preempted with a multiobjective optimization approach that identifies opportunities along the Pareto-optimal frontier to achieve significant gains in some objectives at little sacrifice to others. In this specific example, the formulation of (26) can be updated by putting all of the weight on thevobjective (i.e., minimize the underage penalty) and including a constraint thaty≤ 0.018. In closing, although the currently used Huntington–Hill method and other historical methods are appealing for their simplicity and the fairness criteria they do optimize (Table 1), they do not allow the flexibility to consider other measures of fairness. We would argue that the centuries-old debate on what constitutes fairness in apportionment, and the mere possibility of ties with the current method, suggests a formal multiobjective optimization framework is worth serious consideration.16 The author thanks the editor-in-chief, David Simchi-Levi, for handling this paper, as well as the anonymous associate editor and two referees for their constructive comments throughout. The author also thanks Maurice Queyranne for his valuable feedback and suggestions for reducing the number of nonconvex constraints for the models ofSection 4.2. Thanks also go to Forough Pourhossein for her review and feedback on an earlier draft of this paper.
